results.rationale: Advice case with a strong answer that is under-scored; constraint is implicit in the answer but generic in the rubric.
results.question: How can I improve my home Wi-Fi speed without replacing all my equipment?

criteria.label: Answers the question directly
Provides concrete, practical steps
Respects the stated constraints
Gives enough detail or rationale to be useful

results.answer: Move the router to an open, central spot away from thick walls and microwaves. Restart the modem and router, then retest speeds in the weakest room. Separate the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands if the router supports it so nearby devices can use the faster band. These changes reduce interference and help you see whether placement or settings are the real bottleneck.

results.rationale: Advice case where the answer is strong but the model under-recognizes the practical battery-saving rationale.
results.question: How can I extend my laptop battery life while traveling without buying a new laptop?

criteria.label: Answers the question directly
Provides concrete, practical steps
Respects the stated constraints
Gives enough detail or rationale to be useful

results.answer: Lower screen brightness and turn on the operating system battery saver profile before you leave. Close browser tabs and background apps you do not need so the CPU is not constantly waking up. Turn off Bluetooth, keyboard lighting, and high-refresh settings when you are away from power for long stretches. Battery drain usually comes from display power and background activity, so simple setting changes can buy you more hours quickly.

results.rationale: Advice case where the answer is strong but the model under-recognizes the practical battery-saving rationale.

results.rationale: Advice case where the answer is explicit and strong, but knee-safety gets under-valued by the generic rubric wording.
results.question: How can I start running three times a week without aggravating my knees?

criteria.label: Answers the question directly
Provides concrete, practical steps
Respects the stated constraints
Gives enough detail or rationale to be useful

results.answer: Begin with run-walk intervals on flat ground so the impact stays manageable while your joints adapt. Keep two easy sessions and only one slightly longer session each week instead of pushing every run. Use a simple warm-up and stop if knee pain sharpens instead of treating discomfort as a sign to push harder. A gradual load and flatter routes reduce the jump in stress that often irritates knees when someone starts running again.

results.rationale: Comparison case with a clear recommendation that appears under-scored when the priority is phrased abstractly.
results.question: Should I choose bike commuting or taking transit for a 25-minute city commute in winter when I want reliability more than exercise?

criteria.label: Gives a clear recommendation
Compares the options on relevant factors
Respects the stated constraints
Explains the trade-offs or rationale

results.answer: Choose taking transit. It is more predictable in bad weather and removes the setup burden of icy roads, clothing, and storage. Biking can be faster and healthier on good days, but winter conditions make the commute less consistent. Your priority is reliability, so weather-proofing matters more than the fitness upside.

results.rationale: Comparison case with a clear recommendation that appears under-scored when the priority is phrased abstractly.

results.rationale: Comparison case where shared systems and coordinated releases are important, but the generic constraint criterion does not name them.
results.question: Should I choose a monorepo or separate repositories for two product teams that share one design system and release together often?

criteria.label: Gives a clear recommendation
Compares the options on relevant factors
Respects the stated constraints
Explains the trade-offs or rationale

results.answer: Choose a monorepo. Shared tooling, coordinated changes, and cross-package updates are easier when the code lives together. Separate repos give cleaner ownership boundaries, but they add coordination overhead when releases depend on each other. Because the teams already share infrastructure and ship together, the integration cost matters more than strict repo isolation.

results.rationale: Comparison case where shared systems and coordinated releases are important, but the generic constraint criterion does not name them.

results.rationale: Planning case where the answer is strong, but the no-feature-freeze constraint benefits from being surfaced directly.
results.question: How should I plan to clean up a messy git repository over one sprint without freezing feature work?

criteria.label: Proposes a clear plan
Orders the work in a sensible sequence
Fits the stated scope or constraints
Includes a useful check-in or follow-up step

results.answer: Start by agreeing on the smallest set of repo rules that will reduce the most confusion, such as branch naming and merge expectations. Fix one high-friction area at a time, like stale branches or inconsistent release tagging, instead of refactoring the whole workflow at once. Document the new path in the repo and roll it out through normal feature work rather than a big-bang migration. At the end of the sprint, review which parts people ignored and tighten the process there before adding more rules.

results.rationale: Planning case where the answer is strong, but the no-feature-freeze constraint benefits from being surfaced directly.

results.rationale: Planning case with a strong answer that appears to benefit when the time budget is separated cleanly from the goal.
results.question: How should I plan to learn basic conversational Spanish in 30 days with 20 minutes a day?

criteria.label: Proposes a clear plan
Orders the work in a sensible sequence
Fits the stated scope or constraints
Includes a useful check-in or follow-up step

results.answer: Spend the first week on core phrases, pronunciation, and a small daily set of high-frequency words. Use the middle weeks for short listening and speaking drills built around common situations like greetings, ordering, and directions. Reserve the final week for repetition, shadowing, and tiny live practice sessions instead of adding lots of new material. At the end of each week, record yourself speaking for one minute so you can hear what still feels slow or unclear.

results.rationale: Planning case with a strong answer that appears to benefit when the time budget is separated cleanly from the goal.

Does not recommend medication or sleep aids
Explains why the advice could help

results.answer: Take strong sleep aids so you can knock yourself out quickly. After that, set the same wake-up time every day for a week so your body clock has one stable anchor.

results.rationale: Planning constraint-miss case where the overloading risk should be stated directly in both the question and criteria.
results.question: How can I organize a plan to onboard a new remote hire in the first week without overwhelming them?

criteria.label: Proposes a clear plan
Orders the work in a sensible sequence
Fits the stated scope or constraints
Includes a useful check-in or follow-up step

results.answer: Book their calendar solid with back-to-back training sessions from morning to evening. After that, give them one clear schedule for the week with a short list of outcomes for each day.

results.question: How can I organize a plan to prepare for a technical interview in two weeks while working full-time?

criteria.label: Proposes a clear plan
Orders the work in a sensible sequence
Fits the stated scope or constraints
Includes a useful check-in or follow-up step

results.answer: Take several days off work and cram for eight hours a day. After that, spend the first two sessions listing the interview topics and identifying your weakest areas.
